text: The surgeon enters the abdomen through an incision in the left subcostal region using an optical viewing technique, utilizing six 5mm ports. The cecum and terminal ileum are identified in the right lower quadrant, and the small bowel is run retrograde until abnormal adhesions are observed between loops of small bowel and the small bowel mesentery. The small bowel loop is reflected towards the right abdomen, revealing an abnormal fusion of the left mesocolon and small bowel mesentery. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to separate the left mesocolon from the small bowel mesentery.

After lysing adhesions, the surgeon visualizes the fossa of Lanzert and proceeds with the reduction of multiple loops of small bowel trapped within the hernia sac. The fossa of Lanzert is visible as a congenital space left of the fourth portion of the duodenum, posterior to the left mesocolon, which contains the inferior mesenteric vein. Once the small bowel is completely reduced, the ligament of Treitz is identified by running the small bowel retrograde. The hernia orifice is closed primarily in two layers: the first layer uses a purse string silk suture to approximate the small bowel mesentery, proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon, followed by a second layer of running silk suture to reinforce the approximation.
